airways and routes.                                                29.92 "Hg. Each flight level is stated in three digits that
                                                                   represents hundreds of feet. For example, FL 250 represents
Enhanced Flight Vision System (EFVS). An installed aircraft        an altimeter indication of 25,000 feet.
system which uses an electronic means to provide a display
of the forward external scene topography (the natural or           Floating Waypoints. Floating waypoints represent
manmade features of a place or region especially in a way to       airspace fixes at a point in space not directly associated
show their relative positions and elevation) through the use       with a conventional airway. In many cases, they may be
of imaging sensors, including but not limited to forward-          established for such purposes as ATC metering fixes,
looking infrared, millimeter wave radiometry, millimeter           holding points, RNAV-direct routing, gateway waypoints,
wave radar, or low-light level image intensification. An           STAR origination points leaving the en route structure,
EFVS includes the display element, sensors, computers and          and SID terminating points joining the en route structure.
power supplies, indications, and controls.
                                                                   Fly-by (FB) Waypoint. A waypoint that requires the use of
                                                                   turn anticipation to avoid overshooting the next flight
                                                                   segment.

G-4

Fly-over (FO) Waypoint. A waypoint that precludes any turn       Head-up Guidance System (HGS). A system that projects
until the waypoint is overflown, and is followed by either       critical flight data on a display positioned between the pilot
an intercept maneuver of the next flight segment or direct       and the windscreen. In addition to showing primary flight
flight to the next waypoint.                                     information, the HUD computes an extremely accurate
                                                                 instrument approach and landing guidance solution, and
Four Corner Post Configuration. An arrangement of air            displays the result as a guidance cue for head-up viewing
traffic pathways in a terminal area that brings incoming         by the pilot.
flights over fixes at four corners of the traffic area, while
outbound flights depart between the fixes, thus minimizing       Height Above Touchdown (HAT). The height of the DA
conflicts between arriving and departing traffic.                above touchdown zone elevation (TDZE).

Gateway Fix. A navigational aid or fix where an aircraft         Highway in the Sky (HITS). A graphically intuitive pilot
transitions between the domestic route structure and the         interface system that provides an aircraft operator with all
oceanic route airspace.                                          of the attitude and guidance inputs required to safely fly
                                                                 an aircraft in close conformance to air traffic procedures.

numerical or geometrical quantity or set of such quantities      end of runway (DER) to provide unrestricted climb to at
(mathematical model) that serves as a reference for              least 400 feet above DER elevation.
computing other quantities in a specific geographic region,
such as the latitude and longitude of a point.                   Instrument Approach Waypoint. Fixes used in defining

through the reference datum point (RDP). This angle is           and the APT WP, when required.
published on approach charts (e.g., 3.00°, 3.20°, etc.). GPA
is sometimes referred to as vertical path angle (VPA).           Instrument Landing System (ILS). A precision instrument

adopted by the International Civil Aviation Organization         slope, outer marker, middle marker, and approach lights.
(ICAO) to encompass any independent satellite navigation
system used by a pilot to perform onboard position               Instrument Procedure with Vertical Guidance (IPV). Satellite
determinations from the satellite data.                          or flight management system (FMS) lateral navigation
                                                                 (LNAV) with computed positive vertical guidance based

of more than 25 NM from the centerline of an aircraft’s

Report. This report is also generated by a vertical separation   ICAO is a specialized agency of the United Nations whose
if you are more than 300 feet off your assigned flight level.    objective is to develop standard principles and techniques
                                                                 of international air navigation and to promote development
Ground Communication Outlet (GCO). An unstaffed,                 of civil aviation.
remotely controlled ground/ground communications
facility. Pilots at uncontrolled airports may contact ATC and    Intersection. Typically, the point at which two VOR radial

connection. This lets pilots obtain an instrument clearance      angle for positive indication of position, resulting in a VOR/

(HGS).                                                           length of runway, that is declared available and suitable for

in less than prescribed separation between aircraft, or         Minimum En Route Altitude (MEA). The MEA is the lowest
between an aircraft and a vehicle, pedestrian, or object.       published altitude between radio fixes that assures
                                                                acceptable navigational signal coverage and meets
LPV. See Localizer Performance with Vertical Guidance.          obstacle clearance requirements between those fixes. The

that location.                                                  fixes defining the airway, segment, or route.

Maximum Authorized Altitude (MAA). An MAA is a                  Minimum IFR Altitude (MIA). Minimum altitudes for IFR
published altitude representing the maximum usable              operations are prescribed in 14 CFR Part 91. These MIAs are
altitude or flight level for an airspace structure or route     published on IFR charts and prescribed in 14 CFR Part 95
segment. It is the highest altitude on a Federal airway, jet    for airways and routes, and in 14 CFR Part 97 for standard

which an MEA is designated at which adequate reception
of navigation signals is assured.                               Minimum Navigation Performance Specifications (MNPS).
                                                                A set of standards that require aircraft to have a minimum
Metering Fix. A fix along an established route over which       navigation performance capability in order to operate in
aircraft are metered prior to entering terminal airspace.       MNPS designated airspace. In addition, aircraft must be
Normally, this fix should be established at a distance from     certified by their State of Registry for MNPS operation.

above airport elevation (AAE) or above.                         in MNPS airspace, however, standard oceanic separation
                                                                minima is provided between the non-MNPS aircraft and
Mid-RVR. The RVR readout values obtained from sensors           other traffic.
located midfield of the runway.
                                                                Minimum Obstruction Clearance Altitude (MOCA). The
Mileage Break. A point on a route where the leg segment         MOCA is the lowest published altitude in effect between
mileage ends, and a new leg segment mileage begins, often       radio fixes on VOR airways, off-airway routes, or route
at a route turning point.                                       segments that meets obstacle clearance requirements
                                                                for the entire route segment. This altitude also assures
                                                                acceptable navigational signal coverage only within 22
                                                                NM of a VOR.
G-6

Minimum Reception Altitude (MRA). An MRA is determined              Non-Directional Radio Beacon (NDB). An L/MF or UHF
by FAA flight inspection traversing an entire route of flight       radio beacon transmitting nondirectional signals whereby
to establish the minimum altitude the navigation signal can         the pilot of an aircraft equipped with direction finding
be received for the route and for off-course NAVAID facilities      equipment can determine bearing to or from the radio
that determine a fix. When the MRA at the fix is higher than        beacon and “home”on or track to or from the station. When
the MEA, an MRA is established for the fix, and is the lowest       the radio beacon is installed in conjunction with the ILS
altitude at which an intersection can be determined.                marker, it is normally called a compass locator.

Minimum Safe Altitude (MSA). MSAs are published for                 Non-RNAV DP. A DP whose ground track is based on

a fly-over waypoint.                                                obstacle slope. The slope, known as the OIS, is based on a

waypoints are generated by various means including                geographical locations, or fixes, used to define an area

                                                                                                                          G-11

Waypoint (WP). A predetermined geographical position
used for route/instrument approach definition, progress
reports, published VFR routes, visual reporting points or
points for transitioning and/or circumnavigating controlled
and/or special use airspace, that is defined relative
to a VORTAC station or in terms of latitude/longitude
coordinates.

Wide Area Augmentation System (WAAS). A method of
navigation based on GPS. Ground correction stations transmit
position corrections that enhance system accuracy and add
vertical navigation (VNAV) features.


G-12
